summary: Gruber dissects Microsoft's failed $300 million rebranding campaign featuring Jerry Seinfeld and Bill Gates, arguing it exemplifies the company's fundamental brand problem. He invokes Paul Rand's principle that a logo is less important than what it represents, extending this to brands generally. Microsoft's core products—Windows (seen as a dud with Vista), Office (boring), and Xbox (too small to matter)—give the company nothing compelling to build a brand around. The Seinfeld ads were well-made entertainment but said absolutely nothing, which Gruber argues was actually an accurate reflection of what Microsoft stands for. He contrasts this with Nike's 'Just Do It' and Apple's 'Think Different,' campaigns that created lasting feelings tied to real brand identities. Microsoft's panicked yanking of the ads after just two weeks revealed that the company recognized the spots had inadvertently exposed the emptiness at its core.
key_points: A strong brand must be built on genuine products and experiences—no amount of clever advertising can substitute for substance
Microsoft's product lineup in 2008 offered nothing emotionally compelling to anchor a brand identity around
The Seinfeld ads succeeded as entertainment but failed as branding because they said nothing—a pattern consistent with years of vapid Microsoft campaigns like 'people_ready'
Microsoft's claim that pulling the Seinfeld ads was planned all along is contradicted by the fact that their PR firm, not their ad agency, made the announcement
The ads were too accurate: they captured that Microsoft as a brand stands for nothing
money_quotes: No brand is better or stronger than the products and experiences it represents. A good brand is strong because it is true, not because it is clever.
The problem Microsoft faces today is that they have nothing to hang their brand on.
This ad says nothing, but pretends to say something.
But they 'worked' only insofar as they said nothing and dropped the pretense of saying something. The spots said nothing and reveled in the nothingness.
Just well-made nothing. A couple of million bucks just burned up in well-produced style.
An effective ad simply has to make a point. Some of the best ads, rather than establishing facts or planting ideas, instead create a feeling.
Advertising campaigns which are going according to plan do not need PR firms to assert such.
I suspect what sparked the panic is that the Seinfeld ads were too good, too accurate at capturing just what it is that Microsoft, as a company and brand, stands for: nothing.
key_insight: Microsoft's Seinfeld ad campaign failed not because it was bad entertainment, but because its polished nothingness inadvertently revealed that the company had no authentic brand identity to advertise.
